project.name: jobsync
project.description: JobSync is a self-hosted, open-source job search assistant and application tracker that helps job seekers organize, track, and manage their applications locally. It features EURES/ESCO integration for European job discovery automations, a Connector/Module ACL architecture, and CRM capabilities, built on Next.js 15 and Prisma. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

layers.name: API & Route Handlers
layers.description: Next.js App Router API routes (/api/v1 public Open Host Service + /api internal proxies, scheduler SSE, logos), the edge middleware, and the src/lib/api auth/rate-limit/response toolkit exposing the domain over authenticated, rate-limited HTTP.

layers.name: Presentation Layer
layers.description: React 19 / Shadcn UI components (staging deck, automation wizard, CRM, settings), App Router pages and layouts, client hooks, context providers, i18n dictionaries for 4 locales, the push service worker, and static public assets.

layers.name: Application & Domain Layer
layers.description: Server-actions-as-Repositories per DDD aggregate, domain models and value objects, plus cross-cutting lib services (events, scheduler, notifications, email, push, assets, CRM, auth, encryption, PII redaction, storage, rate-limit).

layers.name: Connector / Integration ACL
layers.description: The src/lib/connector anti-corruption layer and its pluggable Modules (EURES, Arbeitsagentur, JSearch, Ollama/OpenAI/DeepSeek, data-enrichment, reference-data) translating external APIs into domain types.

layers.name: Data Layer
layers.description: Prisma schema, SQLite migrations, the migration-defined tables, and the seed script — the persistence and schema source of truth.

layers.name: Infrastructure Layer
layers.description: Container definitions (Dockerfile, docker-compose), GitHub Actions CI/CD pipelines, and deployment/release/entrypoint shell scripts.

layers.name: Build, Config & Tooling
layers.description: Project-level build, lint, test, and tooling configuration (Next.js, TypeScript, Jest, Playwright, Tailwind, Knip, Lingui, PostCSS) plus dev-environment manifests and package metadata.

layers.name: Documentation & Specifications
layers.description: Markdown documentation, ADRs, conductor tracks, and authoritative Allium domain specifications under specs/.

layers.name: Testing Layer
layers.description: Jest unit/component/integration specs under __tests__, Playwright E2E suites under e2e, and NextAuth/Prisma test mocks under __mocks__.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Begin with the README to understand what JobSync is: a self-hosted, local-first job-search companion that tracks applications, ingests vacancies, and assists the whole search workflow. It frames the feature set (staging pipeline, CRM, EURES/ESCO integration, notifications) and the Next.js 15 / Prisma / Shadcn stack you will see throughout the tour. Use this as the mental map before diving into code.

tour.title: Application Entry Point
tour.description: The root App Router layout is where the running application boots. It sets the HTML language from the user's locale, wires up the theme and locale context providers, and bootstraps client-side error capture. Every page in the app renders inside this shell, so it is the natural place to see how cross-cutting concerns (i18n, theming, error reporting) are established once for the whole tree.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js App Router root layouts are React Server Components that wrap every route. Edge middleware (src/middleware.ts) runs before the layout on every request, making it the right place for security headers, auth redirects, and request sanitization.

tour.title: Internationalization Backbone
tour.description: Building on the locale provider wired into the layout, the i18n barrel is the single most depended-upon module in the codebase (highest fan-in by a wide margin). It re-exports the useTranslations hook, the LocaleProvider, and locale-aware date/number formatters, while the dictionaries supply translations across four locales. Understanding this early explains why nearly every component imports from src/i18n.

tour.title: Domain Models and the Action Result Contract
tour.description: The application follows a DDD style where domain models define the vocabulary and value objects of the system. The Job model encodes salary periods, contact-role collections, and type guards, while ActionResult<T> is the canonical typed envelope every server action returns. These two are foundational: most server actions consume the Job aggregate and hand back an ActionResult, so they form the contract between the domain and the UI.
tour.languageLesson: TypeScript discriminated unions power ActionResult<T>: a success/error tag lets callers narrow the type safely, so the UI can handle both branches exhaustively without casting.

tour.title: Server Actions as Repositories
tour.description: JobSync implements the Repository pattern using Next.js server actions instead of classes. The job actions module is the top fan-out node in the project: it owns list/detail queries, create/update/delete, status transitions with optimistic locking, and the Kanban board. This is the heart of the application layer where domain rules meet persistence, returning the ActionResult contract from Step 4.
tour.languageLesson: Server actions are async functions marked with the 'use server' directive. They run only on the server, can be called directly from client components, and replace the traditional REST controller layer in this codebase.

tour.title: Persistence: Prisma Client and Schema
tour.description: The actions from Step 5 ultimately read and write through Prisma. The db module is a cached client singleton (the third-highest fan-in node) that survives hot-module-reload without exhausting SQLite connections, and the schema is the authoritative data model defining every table, relation, and index. Together they are the persistence source of truth that the entire application layer depends on.
tour.languageLesson: Prisma generates a fully typed client from schema.prisma. The globalThis singleton pattern in db.ts prevents Next.js dev-server HMR from spawning a new client (and a new connection pool) on every reload.

tour.title: Connector / Integration ACL
tour.description: External job sources (EURES, Arbeitsagentur, JSearch) and AI providers (Ollama, OpenAI, DeepSeek) are isolated behind an anti-corruption layer of pluggable Modules. The manifest defines the shared module contract, and the registry is the singleton store standing behind every connector facade. This layer translates messy external APIs into clean domain types so the rest of the app never depends on a vendor's shape.

tour.title: The Vacancy Discovery Pipeline
tour.description: This is where the connector ACL does real work. The discovery runner orchestrates the full automation: search across modules, deduplicate, apply blacklist filters, fetch details, run PII-redacted AI resume matching, and stage candidates. The staged-vacancy actions then manage that pipeline's lifecycle (dismiss, restore, archive, promote-to-job, bulk operations). Together they implement JobSync's signature ingestion-to-application flow.

tour.title: Presentation: The Staging Deck
tour.description: The staging container is the user-facing top of the pipeline from Step 8. It is a high-fan-out orchestrator coordinating list and deck view modes, tabs, pagination, bulk actions, promotion, blocking, and the auto-approve deck state machine. Studying it shows how the React 19 / Shadcn presentation layer consumes server actions and renders the domain to the user.

tour.title: Public API: Open Host Service
tour.description: Beyond the in-app UI, JobSync exposes its domain over an authenticated, rate-limited public REST API. The v1 jobs route provides paginated GET and POST endpoints wrapped in withApiAuth with audit logging and IDOR protection. This is the Open Host Service that lets external tooling integrate with the same Job aggregate the UI uses.

tour.title: Inside Track: Referrals and Network Connections
tour.description: The Inside Track feature lets users capture insider tips and map their personal network. The model defines referral value objects, validators, and a status transition machine; the referral actions record tips and can commit-to-apply (creating a Job in applied status), while personConnection actions manage person-to-person links. This is a complete vertical slice that reuses the model/action/Prisma patterns from earlier steps.

tour.title: Specification-Driven Design with Allium
tour.description: JobSync is built spec-first: Allium specifications under specs/ are the authoritative description of each feature's entities, triggers, and behavioural rules, which the implementation must conform to. The inside-track spec is the source of truth behind the code you just read in Step 11. Reading the spec alongside its implementation shows how the project keeps design intent and code aligned.
tour.languageLesson: Allium is a domain specification language: specs declare entities, rules, and triggers in a structured, checkable form. The paired SQL migration shows how that spec lands in the database via Referral and PersonConnection tables.

tour.title: Containerization and CI/CD
tour.description: Finally, see how JobSync ships. The multi-stage Dockerfile (base, deps, builder, runner) produces a minimal non-root Node 20 Alpine image, docker-compose orchestrates the app service for self-hosting, and the GitHub Actions workflows run the quality gate and publish multi-arch images to GHCR. This capstone connects all the code above to a deployable, self-hostable product.
tour.languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds separate build-time dependencies from the runtime image: the builder stage runs prisma generate and the Next.js build, while the slim runner stage copies only the standalone output, keeping the published image small and running as a non-root user.
